growth_records.cite: Han et al. Ribonuclease toxin RelE1 inhibits growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis through specific cleavage of the ribosomal anti-Shine–Dalgarno region. Nucleic Acids Research (2025). DOI:10.1093/nar/gkaf1070. [PMC12620021]
growth_records.snippet: RelE1 (or RelE1 R65A) was expressed for 1 and 3 h (corresponding to one doubling time) in M. smegmatis, or 4 and 24 h (corresponding to one doubling time) in M. tuberculosis.
growth_records.notes: Doubling time of 3 h for M. smegmatis mc2155 is stated matter-of-factly to justify an induction-time design (not itself a growth curve measured in this experiment); medium/strain/temperature taken from this paper's own Methods (LB+0.05% Tween80, 37°C). growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/3 = 0.231 h-1. Treated as a generic/reference value rather than a directly measured result of this study, hence low confidence.

growth_records.medium: 7H9 broth + Middlebrook ADC growth supplement + carbenicillin + cycloheximide + 0.25% Tween 80
growth_records.cite: Islam et al. Genome editing outcomes reveal mycobacterial NucS participates in a short-patch repair of DNA mismatches. Nucleic Acids Research (2024). DOI:10.1093/nar/gkae402. [PMC11551744]
growth_records.snippet: M. smegmatis have a doubling time of ∼7 h, and this allows mismatched nucleotides to resolve into permanent genetic mutations after replication.
growth_records.notes: Doubling time of ~7 h stated in text as a general property of M. smegmatis (used to justify experimental timing), not presented as a fresh growth-curve measurement from this study's own cultures, though tied to the strain (mc2155) and medium (7H9+ADC) used throughout the paper's methods. growth_rate_per_h = ln2/7 = 0.099 /h.

up.method: reported (whole-cell apparent Vmax_app from Michaelis-Menten non-linear regression fit to gas-chromatography-measured CO depletion kinetics in carbon-starved stationary-phase, 72 h post-OD_max, cultures amended with 6 CO concentrations 50-4000 ppmv)

growth_records.cite: Cordero et al. Atmospheric carbon monoxide oxidation is a widespread mechanism supporting microbial survival. The ISME Journal (2019). DOI:10.1038/s41396-019-0479-8. [PMC6794299]
growth_records.snippet: The apparent kinetic parameters of this activity (Vmax app = 3.13 nmol gdw-1 min-1; Km app = 350 nM; thresholdapp = 43 pM) are consistent with a moderate-affinity, slow-acting enzyme
growth_records.notes: Vmax_app = 3.13 nmol CO gDW-1 min-1 is stated verbatim in the text. Converted to preferred units: 3.13 nmol/gDW/min x 60 min/h / 1e6 nmol per mmol = 1.878e-4 mmol/gDW/h. Measurement is on carbon-starved, non-growing (stationary-phase, 72 h post-OD_max) whole cells, i.e. a survival/respiration rate rather than a growth-coupled substrate uptake rate. Growth medium was HdB + 5.8 mM glycerol (batch, 37 degrees C, 200 rpm shaking = aerobic per Methods); no numeric growth rate/doubling time for glycerol growth is given anywhere in the main text (only qualitative OD/glycerol depletion curve and a figure-only comparison of specific growth rates with/without 20% CO, described only as 'slight increase in doubling time', no value stated), so growth_rate_per_h is left null rather than invented.

growth_records.cite: Warren et al. Mycobacterial helicase Lhr abets resistance to DNA crosslinking agents mitomycin C and cisplatin. Nucleic Acids Research (2023). DOI:10.1093/nar/gkac1222. [PMC9841417]
growth_records.snippet: The Δlhr mutant grew as well as the wild-type strain in liquid culture in 7H9 medium, with doubling times of 155 and 156 min, respectively.
growth_records.notes: Doubling time (156 min = 2.6 h) reported explicitly for wild-type M. smegmatis mc2155 in liquid 7H9 medium. mu = ln2/doubling_time_h = 0.693/2.6 = 0.267 h-1. Temperature 37C inferred from other liquid-culture handling steps described elsewhere in Methods of the same paper (log-phase 7H9 cultures incubated at 37C with shaking); not restated adjacent to this specific sentence.

growth_records.cite: Warren et al. Mycobacterial helicase Lhr abets resistance to DNA crosslinking agents mitomycin C and cisplatin. Nucleic Acids Research (2023). DOI:10.1093/nar/gkac1222. [PMC9841417]
growth_records.snippet: The Δlhr mutant grew as well as the wild-type strain in liquid culture in 7H9 medium, with doubling times of 155 and 156 min, respectively.
growth_records.notes: Doubling time (155 min = 2.5833 h) reported explicitly for Δlhr M. smegmatis mc2155-derived mutant in liquid 7H9 medium. mu = ln2/doubling_time_h = 0.693/2.5833 = 0.268 h-1. Temperature 37C inferred from other liquid-culture handling steps in same paper's Methods; not restated adjacent to this specific sentence.

growth_records.medium: Hartmans de Bont (HdB) minimal medium + 0.2% glycerol

up.method: glycerol consumption monitored over time (Fig 1B) but no numeric specific rate reported

growth_records.snippet: a statistically significant reduction (P < 0.05) in the growth rate of the gylR mutant (µ = 0.0379 ± 0.0037 h−1) was observed compared to WT M. smegmatis (µ = 0.1971 ± 0.002 h−1)
growth_records.notes: WT M. smegmatis mc2155 on HdB minimal + 0.2% glycerol. Reported specific growth rate µ = 0.1971 ± 0.002 h-1 (calculated by authors from OD600 exponential slope: µ=(ln N2 - ln N1)/(T2-T1)). doubling_time = ln2/µ = 0.6931/0.1971 = 3.52 h. Full HdB salt composition given only by reference (28), so medium_composition_stated=false; only stated components (0.2% glycerol carbon source, 0.05% tyloxapol surfactant) listed. No numeric substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported.

growth_records.medium: Hartmans de Bont (HdB) minimal medium + 0.2% glycerol

up.method: gylR mutant consumes glycerol at slower rate (Fig 1B); no numeric specific rate reported

growth_records.snippet: a statistically significant reduction (P < 0.05) in the growth rate of the gylR mutant (µ = 0.0379 ± 0.0037 h−1) was observed compared to WT M. smegmatis (µ = 0.1971 ± 0.002 h−1)
growth_records.notes: gylR frameshift mutant (single nt insertion at bp462 of MSMEG_6757) on HdB minimal + 0.2% glycerol. Reported µ = 0.0379 ± 0.0037 h-1 (OD600 exponential slope). doubling_time = ln2/µ = 0.6931/0.0379 = 18.29 h. Reaches same final yield as WT but slower. Growth rates on glucose/acetate/succinate stated as not significantly different from WT but no numeric µ values given for those, so not recorded. HdB full salt composition by reference only (medium_composition_stated=false).

growth_records.medium: Middlebrook 7H9 basal medium + Tween 80 + glycerol (glycerol-limited chemostat feed)

up.method: calculated: qS = D / Yglycerol = 0.02 h^-1 / 28.3 gDW/mol = 7.067e-4 mol/gDW/h = 0.707 mmol/gDW/h (steady state, mu=D)

sec.method: calculated: qATP = D / YATP = 0.02 / 3.85 gDW/mol = 5.19e-3 mol/gDW/h = 5.19 mmol/gDW/h (bioenergetic ATP utilization rate, informational only)

growth_records.cite: Soom et al. ATP synthesis driven by atmospheric hydrogen concentrations. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2025). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2506353122. [PMC12318194]
growth_records.snippet: Steady-state cultures were grown on minimal salts medium supplemented with limiting concentrations of glycerol, at dilution rates ranging from 0.02 h-1 (doubling time 35 h, very slow growth) to 0.14 h-1 (doubling time 5 h). Yglycerol=28.3;
growth_records.notes: Chemostat, glycerol-limited, M. smegmatis mc2 155, 37C, pH 7.0 +/- 0.2, agitation 400 rpm (aerobic). At steady state mu = D = 0.02 h^-1. Doubling time reported ~35 h; recomputed td = ln2/D = 0.693147/0.02 = 34.66 h (consistent). Table 1 gives Yglycerol = 28.3 g[dry weight]/mol glycerol utilized and YATP = 3.85 g[dry weight]/mol ATP utilized. Glycerol uptake computed as qS = mu/Yxs = D/Yglycerol = 0.02/28.3 = 7.067e-4 mol/gDW/h = 0.707 mmol glycerol/gDW/h. Mass yield Yxs = 28.3/92.09 (glycerol MW) = 0.307 g/g. ATP utilization rate qATP = D/YATP = 0.02/3.85 = 5.19e-3 mol/gDW/h = 5.19 mmol ATP/gDW/h; text states ATP demand ~263 mmol ATP/g biomass (= 1/YATP = 1/3.85 mol/g = 260 mmol/g, consistent). Whole-culture maintenance ATP mATP = 3 mmol ATP/h/gDW (reported, not dilution-rate specific). Medium is commercial Middlebrook 7H9 basal + 0.1% w/v Tween 80 + 0.2% w/v glycerol (individual salt masses of 7H9 base not itemized in text). O2 uptake not reported numerically; residual glycerol + end products assayed but per-product rates only in SI Appendix (not in main text).

growth_records.medium: Middlebrook 7H9 basal medium + Tween 80 + glycerol (glycerol-limited chemostat feed)

up.method: calculated: qS = D / Yglycerol = 0.14 h^-1 / 38.6 gDW/mol = 3.627e-3 mol/gDW/h = 3.63 mmol/gDW/h (steady state, mu=D)

sec.method: calculated: qATP = D / YATP = 0.14 / 4.04 gDW/mol = 3.465e-2 mol/gDW/h = 34.65 mmol/gDW/h (bioenergetic ATP utilization rate, informational only)

growth_records.cite: Soom et al. ATP synthesis driven by atmospheric hydrogen concentrations. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2025). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2506353122. [PMC12318194]
growth_records.snippet: ...to 0.14 h-1 (doubling time 5 h, fast growth). Table 1: Dilution rate 0.14, Yglycerol 38.6, YATP 4.04, membrane potential 72 +/- 6, ZdeltapH 44 +/- 7, pmf 116 +/- 13. Glycerol-limited continuous culture of M. smegmatis.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat, glycerol-limited, M. smegmatis mc2 155, 37C, pH 7.0 +/- 0.2, 400 rpm (aerobic). At steady state mu = D = 0.14 h^-1. Doubling time reported ~5 h; recomputed td = ln2/D = 0.693147/0.14 = 4.95 h (consistent). Table 1: Yglycerol = 38.6 g[dry weight]/mol glycerol utilized, YATP = 4.04 g[dry weight]/mol ATP utilized. Glycerol uptake qS = D/Yglycerol = 0.14/38.6 = 3.627e-3 mol/gDW/h = 3.63 mmol glycerol/gDW/h. Mass yield Yxs = 38.6/92.09 = 0.419 g/g. ATP utilization qATP = D/YATP = 0.14/4.04 = 3.465e-2 mol/gDW/h = 34.65 mmol ATP/gDW/h. Both dilution-rate records share the same medium/temperature/pH; only D, yields and pmf components differ. O2 uptake and specific end-product secretion rates not given numerically in main text (SI Appendix Note S3 / Fig. S6).

growth_records.medium: Hartmans de Bont (HdB) minimal medium + glycerol

up.method: glycerol consumption monitored over growth phases (qualitative in main text; no numeric qS reported)

growth_records.cite: Kropp et al. Atmospheric hydrogen consumption is regulated by glycerol-mediated catabolite repression in mycobacteria. mSystems (2026). DOI:10.1128/msystems.01678-25. [PMC13098282]
growth_records.snippet: a statistically significant reduction (P < 0.05) in the growth rate of the gylR mutant (µ = 0.0379 ± 0.0037 h-1) was observed compared to WT M. smegmatis (µ = 0.1971 ± 0.002 h-1)
growth_records.notes: WT M. smegmatis mc2155 on glycerol (0.2% wt/vol) as sole carbon source. Reported specific growth rate µ = 0.1971 ± 0.002 h-1, computed by authors as µ=(ln N2 - ln N1)/(T2-T1) from OD600. Doubling time td = ln2/µ = 0.6931/0.1971 = 3.52 h. HdB salt base cited (ref 28) but not enumerated in text; stated components glycerol 0.2% wt/vol and tyloxapol 0.05% wt/vol included. Glycerol consumption measured but only shown qualitatively; no numeric substrate uptake flux. No secretion fluxes reported.

growth_records.medium: Hartmans de Bont (HdB) minimal medium + glycerol

up.method: glycerol consumption monitored; mutant consumes glycerol albeit at a slower rate (qualitative; no numeric qS)

growth_records.cite: Kropp et al. Atmospheric hydrogen consumption is regulated by glycerol-mediated catabolite repression in mycobacteria. mSystems (2026). DOI:10.1128/msystems.01678-25. [PMC13098282]
growth_records.snippet: a statistically significant reduction (P < 0.05) in the growth rate of the gylR mutant (µ = 0.0379 ± 0.0037 h-1) was observed compared to WT M. smegmatis (µ = 0.1971 ± 0.002 h-1)
growth_records.notes: gylR frameshift mutant of M. smegmatis mc2155 on glycerol (0.2% wt/vol) sole carbon source. Reported µ = 0.0379 ± 0.0037 h-1 (author-calculated from OD600 via µ=(lnN2-lnN1)/(T2-T1)). Doubling time td = ln2/µ = 0.6931/0.0379 = 18.29 h. Growth on glucose, acetate, succinate was not significantly different from WT but no numeric µ values given (Fig 2A only) so those conditions not recorded. HdB salt base cited (ref 28) not enumerated.

up.method: derived from measured residual glycerol at steady state; per-gDW rate not derivable (no gDW conversion given)
derived: feed 1.25 mM, residual undetectable => ~1.25 mM consumed x D

growth_records.cite: Petridis, Michael et al. Defining the nitrogen regulated transcriptome of Mycobacterium smegmatis using continuous culture. BMC Genomics (2015). DOI:10.1186/s12864-015-2051-x. [PMC4617892]
growth_records.method: reported (chemostat dilution rate D = mu at steady state; td = 5.7 h reported)
growth_records.snippet: M. smegmatis was grown at 50% air saturation and at a dilution rate of 0.12 h-1... Nitrogen-limited cultures (1.25 mM NH4Cl/30 mM glycerol) reached steady-state at an OD600 of 0.8 and no residual ammonium could be detected... The residual c
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (Actinobacteria). At chemostat steady state mu = D = 0.12 /h; doubling time reported as td = 5.7 h (check: ln2/0.12 = 5.78 h). Glycerol uptake computed volumetrically: qS_vol = D*(S_in - S_out) = 0.12/h * (30 - 18) mM = 0.12*12 = 1.44 mmol/L/h. Ammonium uptake: feed 1.25 mM fully consumed (residual undetectable) => 0.12*1.25 = 0.15 mmol/L/h. Per-biomass (mmol/gDW/h) rates NOT derivable because only OD600 (0.8) is given with no OD-to-gDW conversion factor. Temperature 37 C from Methods; agitation 200 rpm for starters; chemostat aeration set to 50% air saturation. pH not numerically stated for chemostat (HdB medium).

up.method: carbon-limited: feed 30 mM glycerol essentially fully consumed (residual near 0) x D
derived: feed 10 mM, residual 0.5-1 mM (~0.75 mM) => ~9.25 mM consumed x D

growth_records.cite: Petridis, Michael et al. Defining the nitrogen regulated transcriptome of Mycobacterium smegmatis using continuous culture. BMC Genomics (2015). DOI:10.1186/s12864-015-2051-x. [PMC4617892]
growth_records.method: reported (chemostat dilution rate D = mu at steady state; td = 5.7 h reported)
growth_records.snippet: In the carbon-limited culture (10 mM NH4Cl/30 mM glycerol), the steady-state-phase was reached after 56 h at an OD600 of 4.3 with the residual ammonium concentration in the medium remaining constant at 0.5-1 mM... A pulse of 1 mM NH4Cl into
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote. Same run design as nitrogen-limited but nitrogen-replete/carbon-limited: mu = D = 0.12 /h; td = 5.7 h. Glycerol is the limiting nutrient so residual ~0; glycerol uptake qS_vol = D*(30 - ~0) = 0.12*30 = 3.6 mmol/L/h. Ammonium uptake: feed 10 mM, residual ~0.75 mM (midpoint of 0.5-1 mM) => 9.25 mM consumed, 0.12*9.25 = 1.11 mmol/L/h. Per-gDW rates not derivable (only OD600 = 4.3 given, no gDW conversion). Temperature 37 C; 50% air saturation; pH not numerically stated.

growth_records.cite: Cox, Robert Ashley et al. Adaptation of Mycobacteria to Growth Conditions: A Theoretical Analysis of Changes in Gene Expression Revealed by Microarrays. PLoS ONE (2013). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0059883. [PMC3625197]
growth_records.snippet: Table 3: Msmeg Chemostat carbon limited; Faster growth rate 0.15 h-1; Slower growth rate 0.01 h-1; Temperature 37 C. Footnote #: growth conditions from [12] Berney et al.
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (fast-growing mycobacterium) chemostat condition, slower rate. mu = D at steady state = 0.01 h-1 (Table 3). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.01 = 69.3 h. Carbon-limited defined medium; full composition NOT stated here. Growth conditions cited FROM Berney et al. (ref 12). No uptake/secretion/MFA rates reported.

growth_records.cite: Cox, Robert Ashley et al. Adaptation of Mycobacteria to Growth Conditions: A Theoretical Analysis of Changes in Gene Expression Revealed by Microarrays. PLoS ONE (2013). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0059883. [PMC3625197]
growth_records.snippet: Table 3: Msmeg Chemostat carbon limited; Faster growth rate 0.15 h-1; Slower growth rate 0.01 h-1; Temperature 37 C. Footnote #: growth conditions from [12] Berney et al.
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (fast-growing mycobacterium) chemostat condition, faster rate; text notes a fifteenfold change in growth rate for Msmeg (0.01 to 0.15 h-1). mu = D at steady state = 0.15 h-1 (Table 3). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.15 = 4.62 h. Carbon-limited defined medium; full composition NOT stated here. Growth conditions cited FROM Berney et al. (ref 12). No uptake/secretion/MFA rates reported.

up.method: derived qS = mu/Yxs (mu=D=0.15/h; Yxs=0.62 g biomass/g glycerol); qS=0.15/0.62=0.242 g/gDW/h; /92.09 g/mol *1000 = 2.63 mmol/gDW/h
Respiration (O2 consumption) monitored to confirm steady state; no absolute qO2 value tabulated

growth_records.cite: Berney M et al. Unique Flexibility in Energy Metabolism Allows Mycobacteria to Combat Starvation and Hypoxia. PLoS ONE (2010). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0008614. [PMC2799521]
growth_records.snippet: M. smegmatis was grown in carbon-limited continuous culture at dilution rates of either 0.15 h-1 (fast) or 0.01 h-1 (slow) that corresponded to doubling times of 4.6 h and 69 h respectively... Yield glycerol (g biomass/g glycerol) 0.15 -> 0
growth_records.notes: Chemostat at steady state so mu=D=0.15/h (Table 1). Measured biomass yield Yglycerol=0.62 g/g. Glycerol uptake derived: qS=mu/Yxs=0.15/0.62=0.2419 g glycerol/gDW/h; glycerol MW 92.09 g/mol -> 0.2419/92.09*1000=2.63 mmol/gDW/h. Membrane potential 64 mV, ZdeltapH 48 mV, deltamuH+ 111 mV at this D. OD600=5.1, CFU 0.7e9/mL.

up.method: derived qS = mu/Yxs (mu=D=0.01/h; Yxs=0.56 g biomass/g glycerol); qS=0.01/0.56=0.01786 g/gDW/h; /92.09 g/mol *1000 = 0.194 mmol/gDW/h

growth_records.cite: Berney M et al. Unique Flexibility in Energy Metabolism Allows Mycobacteria to Combat Starvation and Hypoxia. PLoS ONE (2010). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0008614. [PMC2799521]
growth_records.snippet: dilution rates of either 0.15 h-1 (fast) or 0.01 h-1 (slow) that corresponded to doubling times of 4.6 h and 69 h... Yield glycerol 0.01 -> 0.56 (g biomass/g glycerol).
growth_records.notes: Steady-state chemostat so mu=D=0.01/h (td=69 h). Yglycerol=0.56 g/g (Table 1). Glycerol uptake derived qS=0.01/0.56=0.01786 g/gDW/h /92.09*1000=0.194 mmol/gDW/h. OD600=4.2, CFU 1.5e9/mL, membrane potential 75 mV, deltamuH+ 130 mV. Reduced respiration vs fast-growing cells (qualitative, no absolute qO2).

growth_records.cite: Berney M et al. Unique Flexibility in Energy Metabolism Allows Mycobacteria to Combat Starvation and Hypoxia. PLoS ONE (2010). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0008614. [PMC2799521]
growth_records.snippet: At 0.6% oxygen saturation, the doubling time was greater (86 hours) indicating that the chemostat culture was oxygen-limited.
growth_records.notes: Oxygen-limited hypoxic chemostat. Reported doubling time 86 h; mu=ln2/86=0.00806/h (calculated). No yield or uptake rate tabulated for this condition. An intermediate 2.5% O2 condition maintained the 69 h doubling time (mu~0.01/h). Included as a distinct low-oxygen physiological state.

yield_fit.note: qS = (1/Yxs)·µ + m_s  — linear fit of carbon-source uptake vs growth rate
